policy_statement: Implement the Tarsheed campaign to reduce per-capita consumption of electricity and water (completed 2007).
mechanism_description: The Tarsheed campaign works to reduce per-capita electricity and water consumption, lowering overall resource use and associated emissions from energy supply and water services.
primary_causal_pathway: Same service demand → ↓ electricity & water use → ↓ energy set for supply/treatment → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Households, businesses, and public entities reduce per-capita electricity and water consumption through awareness, norms, and conservation practices. (2) Mediating process: Lower end-use demand reduces upstream production—less electricity generation and less energy-intensive water abstraction, desalination, pumping, and wastewater treatment. (3) Outcome: Reduced energy use translates into lower CO₂ (and other) emissions from the energy system, with additional reductions where water services are particularly energy intensive.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced demand for electricity and water services, leading to lower fuel combustion (and related process emissions) in power generation and water supply/treatment systems.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Behavior-driven conservation reduces per-capita electricity and water consumption, cutting the energy input needed to provide those services and thereby reducing GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the outcome is emissions reduction (Mitigation), the mechanism’s defining lever is using fewer resources/energy for the same service (efficiency/conservation), which aligns best with Resource Efficiency as the primary category.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “conservation campaign” were primarily aimed at drought resilience or emergency curtailment (hazard response), it could shift toward Adaptation; if it mainly drove fuel switching or renewables adoption, it would be Mitigation. Those are not implied by this mechanism description.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy with this mechanism—resource conservation campaigns that reduce per-capita electricity and/or water use—shares the same dominant pathway of reduced resource demand leading to lower upstream energy use and emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., electricity vs water emphasis, demand-response peak reduction vs annual energy savings) require policy-level evidence and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be classified as Mitigation in taxonomies that treat all demand reduction primarily as emissions abatement; however, under the provided categories, the defining mechanism is “same service with less resource/energy input,” so Resource Efficiency is most consistent. If the campaign is explicitly framed as drought/heat emergency preparedness rather than ongoing conservation, Adaptation could become primary.
key_indicators: “Tarsheed Campaign”; “Initiative”; “reduce the percapita consumption of electricity and water”; “campaign”
co_benefits: Reduced desalination/pumping burden and improved water security; lower peak electricity demand during extreme heat; household cost savings; reduced local air pollution from power generation.
instance_edge_case_notes: Classified as information-disclosure because the text only evidences a campaign/initiative; if the campaign included rebates, tariffs, or mandatory measures, the instrument type could shift to incentive-program or pricing-mechanism, but those elements are not present in the verbatim text.
